meta.workDescription: The Occupational Safety Office keeps the work center safe so the 140th Wing mission can continue without interruption. They conduct regular facility inspections to make sure everyone is following OSHA and Air Force safety rules. If an accident does happen, safety professionals investigate to find the exact root cause and track the required fixes to ensure it never happens again. By monitoring safety training, helping shops manage daily risks, and constantly looking out for hazards, they prevent injuries and keep our personnel ready to fly, fight, and win.
meta.references: DAFI 91-202 The Air Force Mishap Prevention Program DAFMAN 91-203 Air Force Consolidated Occupational Safety Instruction DAFI 91-204 Safety Investigations and Reports DAFI 91-207 USAF Traffic Safety Program AFI 90-821 Hazard Communication AFI 90-802 Risk Management DESR6055.09_AFMAN 91-201 Explosive Safety Standards DAFMAN 91-221 Weapons Safety Investigations and Reports DAFMAN 91-224 Occupational Safety Investigations and Reports OSHA Standards 29 CFR 1910.XXX (General Industry) and 29 CFR 1926.XXX (Construction) also apply
meta.riskManagementNotes:
Risk Management (RM) is a continuous and systematic process designed to preserve combat readiness and enhance force protection by proactively identifying, assessing, and mitigating operational hazards. I have structured this framework around four foundational principles: accepting no unnecessary risk, ensuring decisions are made at the appropriate command authority, integrating assessment early into mission planning, and maintaining continuous application throughout the operational lifecycle. This cycle is executed through a structured five-step methodology that begins with the identification of real or potential hazards, followed by a detailed assessment of their probability and severity to determine overall risk levels. Once identified, leaders develop and implement mitigation controls—such as engineering, administrative, or protective measures—and continuously supervise operations to evaluate control efficacy and adjust for emerging variables in the environment.

To facilitate this process, the Joint Risk Assessment Tool (JRAT) serves as the primary web-based, automated platform managed by the U.S. Army Combat Readiness Center (USACRC) for standardizing risk management across the Department of War. This centralized system automates the generation of the DD Form 2977 (Deliberate Risk Assessment Worksheet) and provides specialized portals for each service branch to ensure alignment with joint standards and mishap statistics. I should note that JRAT streamlines the assessment process by offering pre-populated libraries of mission types and hazards, incorporating real-world lessons learned from historical data, and enabling global coordination for joint operations. By leveraging this tool, personnel can ensure their risk assessments are data-driven, compliant, and effectively integrated into the mission planning process to fortify force protection.
meta.trafficSafetyNotes:
• Colorado is known for hazardous weather. Snow, ice, wind and rain can make driving exponentially more hazardous. During inclement weather you are expected to make a personal judgment on whether you can make it to work or not. If you decide the roads are too hazardous, you must call your supervisor to let them know that you will not be reporting for duty. Buckley SFB has a snow line for you to call for additional information, should you need it (720-847-7669). The State of Colorado also runs a road conditions hotline (Dial 511 or visit www.COtrip.org or download the app as a helpful guide with traveling the state during winter months.)
• Drill weekend traffic. On drill weekends the gate tends to back up. As you come over the overpass you may suddenly find yourself looking at the taillight(s) of a stopped car. Go slow over the crest of the overpass until you are sure traffic isn’t backed up.
• The I-25 and 225 can be very congested at times with traffic during peak commute times. Several serious accidents always occur due to sudden highway traffic stopping abruptly. Ensure you pay particular attention to the flow of traffic and remember not to be on your phone while driving. At intersections when the light turns green, take an extra second to look both ways before entering the intersection.
• Denver, as in other towns, has a problem with red light runners. Ensure you take an extra look both ways at all intersections prior to entering – regardless of if you have the green light or not.
• Colorado is notorious for severe animal/vehicle collisions, with deer being one of the main culprits. This is especially true during the fall rut season and during dusk and dawn. Be aware of your surroundings. Remember if an animal does jump out in front of you, hit it straight on. Do not try to swerve out of the way.
• The City of Denver and surrounding cities are growing rapidly, and intoxicated drivers are a real possibility. Try to plan your travel around the dangerous hours – Friday and Saturday nights 12am - 3am.

meta.activeShooter:
If there is an active shooter threat, you may hear shots or an announcement to Lockdown. Evacuate the immediate area, if possible. If evacuation isn’t possible, personnel in the immediate area must find a place to hide where they are less likely to be discovered by the shooter. Move quickly away from the threat and seek shelter inside the nearest room/building which can be locked, remain out of sight. Turn off the lights and be as quiet as possible, turn off cell phone ringer and vibration. When in a safe location call 911 and provide details about the shooter. Once the suspect is neutralized you will receive further guidance from SFS and local PD.

The three things to keep in mind in an active shooter situation: ESCAPE|BARRICADE|FIGHT

ESCAPE
* Consider all points to safely exit the facility
* Don’t put yourself in harm’s way
* Be prepared to fight
BARRICADE
* Secure yourself away from the shooter
* Locks doors, blockade doors and windows, and try not to trap yourself
* Remain silent and call Emergency Personnel
FIGHT
* Fighting the shooter is extremely risky, only fight if you have no other options.
* Commit to the fight and always assist others in an attempt to overwhelm the shooter
* Use items in your immediate area as weapons examples are tools, fire extinguisher, chairs and etc.

selectedModules.title: Hazardous Energy Control
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Train authorized employees on hazardous energy sources, magnitude, and isolation methods; affected employees on the purpose and use of procedures; and other nearby employees on restart/reenergization prohibitions. Retrain when assignments, equipment, processes, or procedures change, or when inspections show gaps.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: DAFMAN 91-203 establishes a formal Hazardous Energy Control Program with dedicated training, documented procedures, and periodic self-inspections before servicing or maintenance work is performed.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and the Safety Office Role 📚

The Hazardous Energy Control program follows DAFI 91-202, DAFMAN 91-203, and OSHA 1910.147 to prevent accidental machine start-ups. The Installation Occupational Safety Office oversees this program to ensure work centers follow the rules. During inspections, safety personnel verify that shops have accurate Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedures and Job Hazard Analyses (JHAs). If a machine is designed poorly and cannot be safely locked out, the Safety Office investigates the issue, assigns it a Risk Assessment Code (RAC), and tracks the necessary fixes through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Machinery Triggers and Maintenance ⚙️

You must never operate a machine without its safety guards in place. Anytime you need to remove or bypass a guard to fix a machine, perform maintenance, or clear a jam, you must use formal LOTO procedures. While normal operators can do basic setups, only qualified technicians should perform complex repairs. If a machine is broken and waiting for a technician, it must be properly tagged "Out of Service" so no one accidentally turns it on.

Execution of Lockout/Tagout Procedures 🛑

Only trained "Authorized Employees" are allowed to apply locks and perform maintenance. "Affected Employees" are the people who normally use the machine or work nearby. To begin LOTO, the Authorized Employee identifies all power sources, notifies the Affected Employees, and shuts the machine down normally. They then turn off the main breakers or valves to cut the power and attach standard locks and tags. The tags must show the worker's name, the date, and the reason for the lockout.

Because turning off the power does not release trapped energy, the worker must also bleed off or block any stored pressure, heat, or mechanical energy. The most critical step is verification: the worker must physically try to turn the machine on to prove it has zero energy, then immediately switch it back to the "off" position. When the work is done, the Authorized Employee must clear away their tools, put all guards back on, and personally remove their own locks and tags before telling everyone the machine is ready to use again.

selectedModules.title: Hazard Communication
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Provide effective training at initial assignment and whenever a new chemical hazard is introduced. Cover where hazardous chemicals are present, the written program, labels and SDS access, how releases are detected, hazard types, protective measures, emergency procedures, and required PPE.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Employees potentially exposed to chemical hazards must complete HAZCOM training upon initial assignment and whenever a new hazard or chemical is introduced or new tasks create new hazards. Supervisors must ensure SDS access for chemicals used in the work process.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and the "Right to Understand" 📚

The Hazard Communication (HAZCOM) program ensures you know about the chemical hazards in your work area and how to protect yourself. Guided by OSHA 1910.1200 and AFI 90-821, this program shifts from simply having the "Right to Know" to the "Right to Understand" what you are working with. The basic rule is simple: no hazardous chemical can be brought into, stored, or used in the shop without a proper warning label and an easy-to-find Safety Data Sheet (SDS). These tools tell you exactly what the health and physical dangers are.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

The Installation Occupational Safety Office works closely with Bioenvironmental Engineering (BE) to keep your shop safe. While BE tests the actual health risks of the chemicals, the Safety Office inspects the work areas to make sure the rules are followed. During these inspections, safety personnel check that chemicals are stored safely, secondary spray bottles or containers have the correct warning labels, and incompatible chemicals (like acids and bleach) are kept separated. They also ensure HAZCOM is included in your unit's training records (JSTO) and track any major chemical safety fixes through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

selectedModules.title: Bloodborne Pathogens
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Train exposed employees at initial assignment and at least annually, with additional training when tasks or procedures create new exposure. Cover disease signs and transmission, the exposure control plan, task recognition, engineering controls, work practices, PPE use and disposal, and Hepatitis B vaccine information.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Any person whose duties involve reasonably anticipated exposure to blood or other potentially infectious material must be trained and enrolled in the Bloodborne Pathogen Program. Supervisors of affected workplaces must establish a written exposure control plan and conduct training.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Universal Precautions 📚

The Bloodborne Pathogens program follows OSHA 1910.1030 and DAFMAN 91-203 rules. The most important rule is "Universal Precautions," which means you must treat all human blood and body fluids as if they are infected with diseases like HIV or Hepatitis. Any shop where workers might be exposed to blood must have a written Exposure Control Plan (ECP). This plan explains how to use safety gear (PPE), where to find sharps containers for needles, and the rule against eating or drinking in work areas. To warn everyone of the danger, all waste and containers must have the biohazard symbol or be kept in red bags.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

While the base clinic handles medical care and vaccines, the Installation Occupational Safety Office audits the work center to make sure safety rules are being followed. During inspections, safety personnel check that sharps containers are not overfilled, biohazard labels are correct, and the right PPE is available for everyone. If a worker is stuck by a needle or splashed with body fluids, the Safety Office investigates the incident as a workplace accident. They help find the root cause and track any needed safety improvements through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Workers must receive BBP training before they start their job and then once every year after that. This training must be documented in the unit's training records (JSTO). The training covers how diseases spread, how to use PPE correctly, and exactly what to do if an exposure happens. Workers must also be informed that they have the right to get the Hepatitis B vaccine for free and understand the medical steps to take if they are accidentally exposed to blood while working.

selectedModules.title: Hearing Conservation
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Provide a hearing conservation training program for employees exposed at or above the action level, repeat it annually, and update it when equipment or work processes change. Cover hearing effects, protector selection and care, and the purpose of audiometric testing.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Hazardous noise concerns must be evaluated and controls recommended through the occupational health risk assessment process. Use the AF Hearing Conservation Program as the training and control basis when shop noise hazards are identified.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Noise Hazard Markings 📚

The Hearing Conservation Program prevents permanent hearing loss and is governed by AFI 48-127 and OSHA rules. Any work area where average noise reaches 85 decibels, or where sudden impact noises hit 140 decibels, is officially a hazardous noise area. To clearly communicate this danger, supervisors must post warning signs at all entrances to these areas and directly on loud machinery. These signs must clearly state that hearing protection is required, and supervisors are responsible for strictly enforcing its use.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

Managing noise hazards is a team effort. While Bioenvironmental Engineering measures the actual noise levels and Public Health tests workers' hearing, the Installation Occupational Safety Office ensures the safety rules are actively followed. During facility inspections, safety personnel check that required warning signs are posted and that workers are properly wearing their ear protection. If an area is too loud, the Safety Office works with the shop to install fixes—like sound-absorbing panels or equipment enclosures—and tracks these facility upgrades through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP). They also investigate the root cause if a worker suffers job-related hearing loss.

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Supervisors must provide initial and annual hearing safety training for any worker exposed to hazardous noise. This training must be documented in the unit's training records (JSTO). Workers must learn exactly how noise damages their ears and how to properly choose, insert, and wear different types of hearing protection (such as custom earplugs or noise-blocking headsets). Finally, the training must emphasize how critical it is for workers to attend their annual hearing tests with Public Health so any early signs of hearing loss can be caught before the damage becomes permanent.

selectedModules.title: Manual and Powered Hoists
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: When hoisting operations involve overhead or gantry cranes, instruct personnel involved in multi-crane lifts on positioning, rigging, and movement coordination under a qualified person, and ensure operators are familiar with the operation and care of fire extinguishers provided. For crawler, locomotive, and truck cranes, make operating and maintenance personnel familiar with extinguisher use and local crane procedures.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: MHE operator qualification and training must be provided by the responsible organization using lesson plans for each equipment type that include formal instruction, hands-on demonstrations, practical exercises, and evaluation. Forklift operators must be evaluated at least once every three years and retrained after unsafe operation, accidents, different equipment assignments, or workplace changes.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Hazard Markings 📚

The safe use of manual and powered hoists is governed by DAFMAN 91-203 (Chapters 12 and 35) and OSHA rules to prevent dropped loads and crushing injuries. Every hoist and crane must clearly display its maximum weight limit on each side, and these numbers must be readable from the ground. If a hoist has multiple lift points, each one must have its own rating. Operators must never lift more than this weight limit. Additionally, powered hoists must have working warning alarms, and dangerous areas—like the swing radius of a crane—must be marked off with barricades or signs to keep bystanders safe.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

While Civil Engineering or contractors handle major maintenance and annual load testing, the Installation Occupational Safety Office audits the work center to ensure everyday safety rules are followed. During inspections, safety personnel verify that annual certifications are up to date, weight limits are properly posted, and daily pre-use checklists are being used. If they find severe safety defects—such as frayed wire ropes, bent hooks, or broken limit switches—the Safety Office will stop operations. They make sure the broken hoist is locked and tagged "Out of Service" and track the required repairs through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Only designated and fully trained personnel are allowed to operate hoists. Supervisors must document this training in the unit's training records (JSTO). Training must cover how to safely use the specific equipment, standard hand signals, proper rigging techniques, and the strict rule to never move or suspend a load over people. Before using a hoist on any given shift, the operator must perform a thorough pre-use inspection. They must physically and visually check the hooks, ropes, chains, safety switches, and brakes. If any damage is found, the operator must immediately tag the hoist out of service until a qualified technician repairs it.

selectedModules.title: Elevated Work Platforms
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Only allow operation by personnel proficient in the operation, safe use, and inspection of the specific platform being used. If a personal fall arrest system is part of the setup, train users on safe system use before first use and after system changes.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: All lift training must be performed by a qualified trainer/supervisor and documented by model. Training plans must include classroom and hands-on proficiency training, inspection requirements, hazard recognition, safety devices, workplace/site inspection, spotter duties, written testing, and model-specific authorization.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Hazard Markings 📚

The use of Elevated Work Platforms (EWPs), such as boom and scissor lifts, is governed by DAFMAN 91-203 (Chapter 16) and OSHA rules to prevent falls, tipping, and electrocution. Every lift must clearly display its maximum weight limit and safety warning stickers (like power line clearances). The manufacturer’s manual must also be kept on the lift at all times. Before raising the platform, workers must set up warning signs, barricades, or caution tape on the ground. This creates a safe "drop zone" that warns people to stay away and protects bystanders from falling tools or debris.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

Because working at heights is highly dangerous, the Installation Occupational Safety Office closely audits EWP operations. During inspections, safety personnel check that units have a written Rescue Plan and review their hazard assessments. They also verify that fall protection harnesses are in good condition and that daily lift inspection logs are being filled out. If a lift has broken parts—such as leaking hydraulics, damaged guardrails, or bypassed safety switches—the Safety Office will stop work, tag the equipment "Out of Service," and track the required repairs through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Operating an EWP safely requires hands-on training that must be documented in the unit's training records (JSTO). Operators must be trained on the exact make and model of the lift they will be using. If operating a boom lift, workers must also receive fall protection training to learn how to inspect their harnesses, connect to approved anchor points inside the basket, and execute the unit's rescue plan if a fall happens. Before starting any work, operators are required to perform a full daily inspection of the vehicle (tires, ground controls, hydraulics) and the lift basket (upper controls, guardrails) to ensure the machine is completely safe to use.

selectedModules.title: Fall Arrest Systems
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Train personnel in safe personal fall arrest system use before use and after any component or system change. Pair this with the AF and local fall protection requirements for inspection, anchorage, rescue, and user limitations.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Any work above four feet requires a fall protection program and appropriate safety gear. Use the local fall protection program, rescue procedures, and chapter-specific AF requirements for application-level training.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Equipment Warnings 📚

Fall protection rules under DAFMAN 91-203 (Chapter 13) and OSHA standards are designed to stop fatal falls. A Personal Fall Arrest System (PFAS) includes a certified anchor, a lanyard or lifeline, and a full-body harness. All gear must have readable labels showing weight limits and safety ratings. If a label is missing or unreadable, the equipment must be removed from service. Additionally, any unprotected ledge or drop of 4 feet or more must be marked with barricades or warning signs to communicate the danger and keep unauthorized personnel away from the edge.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

Because falls are a high-risk hazard, the Installation Occupational Safety Office strictly audits unit fall protection programs to ensure everyone is following the rules. Safety professionals review unit-level Fall Protection and Rescue Plans to ensure they are site-specific and meet Air Force requirements. During inspections, they check gear to verify that it is safe and that a designated "Competent Person" is performing regular documented inspections. While Civil Engineering (CE) is responsible for certifying structural anchor points, the Safety Office verifies that these certifications are current before any work begins. If a shop is found using unsafe gear or lacks a rescue plan, the Safety Office can stop work immediately, tag the equipment out of service, and track the necessary fixes through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Using fall protection gear correctly requires hands-on training for anyone designated as an "Authorized Person." This training must be documented in the unit's Job Safety Training Outline (JSTO) and provided by a qualified "Competent Person." Personnel must learn how to perform daily pre-use inspections to spot damaged webbing, bent D-rings, or deployed shock absorbers before they put the gear on. Training also covers how to calculate total fall clearance to ensure a worker will not hit the ground or objects below before the system catches them. Most importantly, everyone must be trained to immediately execute the unit's Rescue Plan to save a suspended coworker and prevent life-threatening suspension trauma.

selectedModules.title: Forklift / Material Handling Equipment
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Ensure each operator is trained and evaluated before operating independently. Training must combine formal instruction, practical training, and workplace evaluation, cover truck-specific and site-specific hazards, and include refresher training after unsafe operation, incidents, assignment to a different truck, or workplace changes.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: MHE training must follow equipment-specific lesson plans with formal instruction, hands-on demonstrations, exercises, and evaluation. Around aircraft, only licensed drivers will operate forklifts and hi-lift trucks, special training is required for hi-lift operators, and spotters must be used when maneuvering close to aircraft or raising and lowering cargo beds.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Equipment Markings 📚  

Forklift and Material Handling Equipment (MHE) operations are governed by DAFMAN 91-203 (Chapter 35) and OSHA 1910.178 to prevent tip-overs, dropped loads, and injuries. Every forklift must have a readable manufacturer’s data plate showing its maximum load capacity and weight. If this plate is missing or unreadable, the forklift cannot be used. Additionally, support areas like battery charging or refueling stations must have clear warning signs for flammable or corrosive hazards, "no smoking" rules, and easy access to Safety Data Sheets (SDS) and eyewash stations.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️ 

The Installation Occupational Safety Office audits work areas to ensure forklift operations are safe. They check for marked pedestrian walkways, good lighting, and mirrors at blind corners to prevent crashes. They also inspect refueling areas for proper ventilation and hazard signs. If safety personnel see dangerous habits—like operators not wearing seatbelts or driving with blocked views—they can stop work. If an accident happens, they investigate the cause, remove broken equipment from service, and track facility safety fixes through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Forklift operators must complete a strict training program before getting licensed. This includes classroom learning, hands-on practice, and a driving test. Training must cover vehicle stability, safe lifting, and mandatory seatbelt use, and it must be recorded in the unit's training files (JSTO). Operators are also required to perform and document a daily pre-use inspection (typically using an AF Form 1800). If they find a major defect—such as bad brakes, hydraulic leaks, or a broken horn—they must immediately tag the forklift "Out of Service" until a qualified mechanic fixes it.

selectedModules.title: Explosives Safety Training
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Use explosives-specific AF lesson plans and local operating instructions. OSHA 2254 includes transportation and explosives references, but the Air Force explosives program should remain the primary training basis for this module.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Explosives Markings 📚

Explosives safety is governed by DAFMAN 91-201 and DAFI 91-202 to protect people and equipment from accidental blasts. To communicate hazards clearly, all explosives storage and work areas must display standard DoD Fire Symbols and Hazard Class placards (like HD 1.1 or 1.4) on the outside of the building. This helps emergency responders immediately know the danger level inside. If munitions contain chemicals, like white phosphorus, chemical warning signs and Safety Data Sheets (SDS) must also be easy to reach.

The Role of the Installation Safety Office 🛡️

The Installation Safety Office uses a team approach to manage explosives areas. The Weapons Safety Manager (WSM) is responsible for the technical explosive limits and site planning. The Occupational Safety Office handles the industrial side, making sure forklifts used to move munitions are safe and that "Lockout/Tagout" rules are followed on assembly machines. Both offices work together to investigate accidents and track safety improvements through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Handling explosives is high-risk, so training is mandatory under DAFMAN 91-201. Personnel must finish initial training before they are allowed to work unsupervised and must take a refresher course every 15 months. This training focuses on local base rules, how to handle specific munitions, and how to use grounding and bonding to prevent static sparks. All training must be documented in official records to ensure only qualified people handle explosives.

selectedModules.title: CPR Training
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Where work conditions require trained first-aid responders, ensure personnel are adequately trained and available. OSHA 2254 links first-aid and CPR availability to certain work settings, but local mission risk and AF requirements should define who must hold current CPR credentials.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Initial first aid and CPR training must be completed before assigning duties that require it, refresher training must occur before certification expires, and CPR training must include PAD instruction. Remote or high-risk work must have an immediate medical response plan.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Emergency Markings 📚

DAFMAN 91-203 (Chapter 1, para. 1.8) and OSHA rules (29 CFR 1910.151 and 1910.269) require work centers to be ready for medical emergencies. This is especially critical for high-risk tasks like high-voltage electrical work, confined space entry, or when a medical facility is not close by. To properly communicate emergency information, all first aid kits, emergency showers, and AEDs must be marked with clear, highly visible signs. Safety bulletin boards must also display emergency contact numbers, the building's physical address for 911 dispatch, and the names of on-duty CPR-trained personnel.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

While the base clinic provides actual medical care, the Installation Occupational Safety Office audits work centers to ensure they are prepared for emergencies. During annual inspections, safety personnel review unit hazard assessments to see if the shop's specific jobs legally require CPR-trained personnel on every shift. They also inspect first aid kits to make sure they are stocked and unexpired, and verify that AEDs are working and properly labeled. If a severe workplace injury occurs, the Safety Office investigates how well the emergency response worked and tracks any needed fixes through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Supervisors of high-risk shops must ensure enough workers are certified in CPR and basic first aid through recognized groups like the American Red Cross or American Heart Association. This training must include hands-on practice; online-only classes are not allowed for meeting OSHA high-risk standards. For jobs involving high voltage, a CPR-trained employee must be immediately available at the work site. Supervisors must track all CPR certifications in the unit's training records (JSTO) to ensure they do not expire. Lastly, these designated responders must also receive Bloodborne Pathogens training so they can safely provide aid without risking infection.

selectedModules.title: Flight Line Driving
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Use flight-line-specific AF lesson plans and local airfield driving rules. OSHA 2254 does not provide a direct flight-line driving training excerpt for this module.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Use Chapter 24 flightline vehicle operations rules and local licensing programs. For specialized equipment like forklifts and hi-lifts around aircraft, only licensed drivers may operate and the owning organization must provide special training with spotter support as required.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Airfield Hazards 📚

Driving on the flight line is dangerous and strictly controlled by DAFMAN 91-203, Chapter 24 (specifically paragraphs 24.13 and 24.13.10), as well as local base rules. The main goal is to prevent crashes and keep aircraft safe, as aircraft always have the right-of-way. On the airfield, "hazard communication" does not just mean chemical warning labels; it means paying attention to pavement markings, warning signs, and vehicle lights. Drivers must know how to read runway hold lines, Instrument Landing System (ILS) signs, and Foreign Object Damage (FOD) checkpoint boundaries. Vehicles must also use rotating beacons, headlights, and reflective markings to communicate their presence to pilots and the air traffic control tower.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

Airfield Management controls the physical flight line, but the Installation Occupational Safety Office audits work centers to make sure drivers are following safety rules. During inspections, safety personnel check that drivers have a valid AF Form 483 (flight line license) and watch for unsafe habits, like speeding, failing to use a spotter when backing up, or forgetting to use wheel chocks. If a vehicle hits an aircraft or ground equipment, the Safety Office investigates the crash to find the root cause. If they find an airfield hazard, like faded pavement lines, they track the repair through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Because every base layout is different, flight line driving training must follow local procedures. Supervisors must make sure personnel complete all classroom requirements, which usually include computer-based training, a color vision test, and a briefing from Airfield Management. After that, drivers must complete hands-on practical training during both the day and night with a qualified instructor, finishing with a final driving test. This training is documented on the AF Form 483 and in unit training records. Drivers must fully understand local speed limits, FOD prevention, night driving rules, and how to read light gun signals from the control tower if their radio breaks.

selectedModules.title: Fetal Protection Program
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Use AF occupational and public health guidance to brief reproductive hazards, reporting expectations, and referral procedures. No direct OSHA 2254 module is mapped here.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Reproductive Hazard Warnings 📚

The Fetal Protection Program is guided by AFMAN 48-146 to protect unborn children from workplace dangers like toxic chemicals, radiation, or extreme physical stress. Applying hazard communication rules to fetal protection means you must know which materials in your shop are "reproductive hazards." You can find this information by reading the Safety Data Sheets (SDS) and looking for specific warning labels—like the health hazard pictogram (a person with a starburst on their chest)—on chemical containers. These labels clearly communicate if a product can harm a developing baby.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

While Public Health and medical providers handle the medical side of pregnancy, the Installation Occupational Safety Office works to ensure the physical work environment is safe. Safety professionals team up with Bioenvironmental Engineering (BE) to verify that reproductive hazards are properly labeled, stored, and controlled. If a pregnant worker's normal duties expose them to dangerous chemicals or severe physical hazards, the Safety Office assists supervisors in finding safe alternatives—such as improving ventilation, providing different protective gear, or recommending administrative changes to the worker's daily tasks.

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Workers must be briefed on any specific reproductive hazards in their shop as part of their initial safety training, which is tracked in the unit's records (JSTO). The most critical part of this program is communication: if a worker becomes pregnant, they are highly encouraged to voluntarily report their pregnancy to Public Health as early as possible. Once reported, Public Health and BE will review the worker's specific job duties and create a customized safety profile. This profile gives the supervisor clear, medical-based guidelines on what tasks the worker can safely perform and what hazards they must avoid for the duration of the pregnancy.

selectedModules.title: Ladder Safety
selectedModules.reference: Common local training item often broken out separately with fixed and portable ladder rules
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Use local ladder lesson plans, manufacturer instructions, and AF walking-working surface guidance. OSHA 2254 (2015 edition) does not provide a dedicated general-industry ladder training summary in the same way it does for lockout, forklifts, or confined spaces, so this remains primarily AF/local-driven.
selectedModules.trainingSource: No direct OSHA 2254 training excerpt; local procedures
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Personnel using ladders at any working height must be trained when first assigned, and that training must include hands-on instruction covering ladder defects, electrocution hazards, positioning, and placement for various job sites. Training must be documented under DAFI 91-202.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Hazard Markings 📚

Using ladders safely is guided by DAFMAN 91-203 to prevent dangerous falls in the work center. Applying hazard communication rules to ladders means that every ladder must have clear, readable manufacturer safety labels. These stickers communicate vital information, such as the maximum weight capacity, electrical hazards (like avoiding metal ladders near power lines), and explicit warnings indicating which top steps are unsafe to stand on. If these labels are missing, torn, or covered in paint, the ladder is non-compliant and should be removed from service.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

The Installation Occupational Safety Office routinely checks for ladder hazards during their shop inspections. Safety professionals verify that ladders are stored safely so they don't fall over, and they look for dangerous habits—like workers standing on the very top cap or overreaching instead of moving the ladder. If safety personnel find a ladder with bent steps, missing rivets, or broken anti-slip feet, they will make sure it is immediately tagged "Out of Service" and destroyed so no one accidentally uses it. Any major safety trends are tracked through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Before you use a ladder, you must receive basic ladder safety training, which is documented in your unit’s training records (JSTO). The most important rule is the pre-use inspection: you must physically check the ladder for damage before climbing it. When using the ladder, it must be placed on a firm, level surface. You must always maintain "three points of contact" (such as two hands and one foot) while climbing up or down. Finally, never overreach while working—keep your belt buckle between the side rails, and never stand on the top step of a stepladder.

selectedModules.title: Active Shooter Response
selectedModules.reference: Local emergency response briefing and lockdown procedures
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Tie this to the local emergency action plan, shelter/lockdown locations, notification methods, and commander-directed response procedures.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Emergency Alerts 📚

Surviving an active shooter event relies on your work center's Local Emergency Action Plan (EAP). In an active threat situation, "hazard communication" means paying immediate attention to mass notification systems. This includes listening to the base Giant Voice, reading AtHoc computer pop-ups, and checking official text alerts. These systems are designed to communicate the danger instantly, telling you exactly where the threat is and whether your building needs to lock down or evacuate.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

While Security Forces and local law enforcement handle the actual threat, the Installation Occupational Safety Office makes sure your work center is prepared beforehand. During their routine facility inspections, safety professionals check your shop's Emergency Action Plan to ensure it is accurate and up to date. They also physically inspect the workspace to make sure all emergency exit doors and escape routes are clear of boxes or equipment, ensuring everyone has a fast, unobstructed path to escape if they need to run.

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Every worker must be trained on the local active shooter procedures as soon as they arrive at the unit, and this training must be documented in the shop's training records (JSTO). The core strategy is always "Run, Hide, Fight." If there is a safe path, Run away from the danger to a secure rally point. If you cannot escape, Hide by locking the door, barricading it with heavy furniture, and silencing your phone. If your life is in immediate danger and you have no other choice, Fight back using whatever tools you have in the shop to stop the shooter.

selectedModules.title: Government Vehicle / Utility Vehicle Use
selectedModules.reference: Local GOV, GVO, or UTV operating rules and licensing expectations
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Document local licensing, route restrictions, spotter use, rollover protection, and unit-specific operating rules. OSHA 2254 does not provide a direct GOV/UTV training excerpt here.
selectedModules.trainingSource: No direct OSHA 2254 training excerpt; local procedures
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Vehicle Markings 📚

Operating government vehicles (GOV) or utility vehicles (UTV) is for official business only. Under local rules and Air Force safety standards, you must follow all speed limits and always wear your seatbelt. To communicate safety rules clearly, these vehicles must have "Official Use Only" markings and clearly posted speed limit or warning stickers inside the cab. Any specific hazards—such as towing limits, weight capacities, or the required fuel type—must also be clearly labeled on the vehicle so the driver knows how to operate it safely.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

The Installation Occupational Safety Office monitors vehicle safety during their routine base inspections. They watch for safe driving habits and ensure that UTVs are not being overloaded or used in ways that could cause a tip-over. If a vehicle is involved in a crash or a "close call," the Safety Office investigates the cause to see if it was due to human error or a mechanical failure. They track any needed safety improvements, such as better road markings or vehicle modifications, through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Before you can drive a GOV or UTV, you must be properly trained and licensed (carrying an AF Form 2293). This training must be documented in your unit’s records (JSTO). Every time you use a vehicle, you are required to perform a pre-use inspection—typically using an AF Form 1800—to check the brakes, tires, lights, and fluid levels. If you find any safety issues that make the vehicle dangerous to drive, you must tag it "Out of Service" and report it to vehicle maintenance immediately so it can be fixed.

selectedModules.title: Manual Lifting and Material Handling
selectedModules.reference: Manual lifting techniques and available lifting devices
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Cover shop-specific lifting limits, two-person lift triggers, hand truck and lifting-device use, and when supervisors require assisted handling. No direct OSHA 2254 standalone manual-lifting module is mapped here.
selectedModules.trainingSource: No direct OSHA 2254 training excerpt; local procedures
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Supervisors must ensure thorough training on proper manual lifting techniques, required PPE, and the use of manual lifting devices. Training must use both verbal and written materials, include practice on proper motions, and be documented under DAFI 91-202.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Lifting Hazard Markings 📚

To prevent back injuries and muscle strains, manual lifting and material handling are guided by DAFMAN 91-203 (paragraph 2.10.3) and local shop rules. Before picking anything up, you must always assess how heavy and awkward the object is. Applying hazard communication to material handling means that heavy boxes, crates, or equipment should be clearly marked with their total weight or display brightly colored "Team Lift" warning stickers. This visual warning immediately communicates the lifting hazard to workers before they attempt to move the item.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

The Installation Occupational Safety Office monitors the work center for ergonomic hazards that can cause severe, long-term injuries. During routine safety walk-throughs, safety professionals check that heavy items are safely stored at waist height rather than on the floor, and that workers have easy access to mechanical lifting aids like hand trucks, dollies, or pallet jacks. If a work center experiences a high number of lifting-related injuries, the Safety Office will investigate the workspace layout, recommend better material handling equipment, and track those ergonomic upgrades through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Workers must be trained on safe kinetic lifting techniques as soon as they are assigned to the shop. This instruction must be formally documented in the unit's Job Safety Training Outline (JSTO). The training covers the absolute basics of safe lifting: bend at your knees, keep your back straight, hold the load close to your body, and never twist your spine while carrying an object. The most critical rule is that if a load is too heavy, too bulky, or blocks your vision, you must stop and get a coworker to help or use mechanical equipment to move it safely.

selectedModules.title: Fire Protection and Prevention
selectedModules.reference: Local extinguisher, pull station, and fire reporting procedures
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Review the fire prevention plan with employees when the plan is developed, when responsibilities change, and when the plan changes. If extinguishers are provided for employee use, train employees on the general principles of extinguisher use and the hazards of incipient-stage firefighting at initial assignment and at least annually. Train designated users on the specific equipment they are expected to use.
selectedModules.afTrainingRequirement: Facility managers and supervisors must establish and maintain a fire prevention training program through the JSTO so employees understand their fire prevention and protection responsibilities in their work areas. This is fulfilled through job safety training and documentation under DAFI 91-202.
selectedModules.notes:
Rules and Fire Hazard Markings 📚

To keep the work center safe from fires, operations must follow DAFMAN 91-203 (paragraph 6.2.1.1) and OSHA standards (1910.39 and 1910.157). Every shop must have a clear Fire Prevention Plan that outlines how to handle trash, store chemicals, and safely evacuate in an emergency. Applying hazard communication rules to fire safety means all flammable liquids must be kept in approved cabinets with clear warning labels, and Safety Data Sheets (SDS) must be easy to find. Additionally, fire extinguishers and emergency exits must be highly visible, properly labeled with signs, and never blocked by equipment or boxes.

The Role of the Occupational Safety Office 🛡️

While the Base Fire Department handles major fire codes and actual emergencies, the Installation Occupational Safety Office audits everyday fire safety during their routine facility inspections. Safety professionals walk through the shop to make sure emergency exit routes are completely clear and that workers are doing their monthly visual checks on fire extinguishers. They also look at how flammable chemicals are stored to prevent accidental fires. If they find serious fire hazards—like chained exit doors or overloaded electrical outlets—they will stop the unsafe practice and track the required fixes through the Master Hazard Abatement Plan (MHAP).

Execution and Training Requirements 📋

Workers must be trained on the shop's specific fire hazards and emergency evacuation routes as soon as they are assigned to the work center. If supervisors expect workers to use portable fire extinguishers to put out small fires, those workers must receive specific, hands-on training (like using the P.A.S.S. method) before they are allowed to use them. All fire safety and evacuation training must be documented in the unit's Job Safety Training Outline (JSTO) so everyone knows exactly what to do when an alarm sounds.

selectedModules.title: Jewelry / Loose Articles Restrictions
selectedModules.reference: Workplace-specific limits on rings, necklaces, watches, and loose items
selectedModules.trainingRequirement: Use local shop rules and hazard analysis to define where jewelry, loose clothing, or unsecured items are prohibited around moving parts, climbing, electrical work, or snag hazards.
selectedModules.trainingSource: No direct OSHA 2254 training excerpt; local procedures
selectedModules.notes: Wearing jewelry or loose clothing around machinery is a major safety hazard. To prevent your rings, watches, or necklaces from getting caught in moving parts or causing an electrical shock, you must remove them before starting work. Local shop rules and DAFMAN 91-203 require these restrictions to protect you from severe injuries, such as "degloving" or entanglement. The Occupational Safety Office helps identify these high-risk areas and ensures that "no-jewelry" warning signs are posted at shop entrances to clearly communicate the danger. During their inspections, safety personnel check for compliance to make sure these simple rules are followed to prevent avoidable accidents.
